other_observers: Doug Faulkner; Doug was with me and he found the bird in a flock of about 45 ibis.
specific_location: One mile east of Maybell on U.S. 40
description: The bird was obviously a Plegadis ibis, with a long, decurved bill, rusty/green plumage, fairly long legs, and hunch-backed posture. The bill was brown and the eyes were also brown. The facial skin was blue and showed no evidence of purplish or pink as would be shown in a hybrid. The borders to the blue facial skin were a light blue, narrow, and extended from the base of the bill to the eye. Body plumage was rusty colored, save for iridescent green/purple across the lower mantle, wings, and tertials. No bronze color was present in the tertials. The legs were dark gray with contrasting red tibia-tarsal joints. The bird was also the largest ibis in the flock, which sometimes can be a useful tool.Behaviors: Actively foraged in wet meadow. Usually had head down and foraged in a swinging motion. Also observed in flight.Call: None heardPlumage: adult alternate
